jokes.text: All right, one for 'Resident.' - That's me! - Well, that's it. One stinkin' letter? Why'd you make us gather round like that? I needed my power fix.
jokes.setup: Mail carrier dramatically calls everyone to gather for mail delivery
jokes.punchline: Admits he just needed his power fix after delivering one generic letter
jokes.explanation: Great character moment - the mail carrier's petty power trip is perfectly human and specific. Efficient structure with strong payoff.

jokes.text: Mom, they put every kid in America in that book... just so gullible parents will buy it. It's all a big scam. - [Muttering] Shut up.
jokes.setup: Lisa explains the obvious scam to her parents
jokes.punchline: Homer mutters 'shut up' because he wants his reward despite knowing it's fake
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer - he knows Lisa is right but doesn't want reality to interfere with getting a reward.

jokes.text: Are you sure? I can usually smell a scam from two towns over. - Yeah, Lise. She is a smart, sophisticated woman.
jokes.setup: Marge claims she can smell scams from two towns away
jokes.punchline: Homer sarcastically calls her smart and sophisticated while she's being obviously gullible
jokes.explanation: Standard Homer sarcasm. Works but not particularly memorable or innovative.

jokes.text: [Grunts] Man, it feels good to get out of that car.
jokes.setup: They've driven to a go-kart track
jokes.punchline: Homer acts like he's been trapped in the car for hours when they just arrived
jokes.explanation: Mild absurdist humor. Homer treating a normal car ride like an ordeal is classic but unremarkable.

jokes.text: [Laughing] Look at me, Bart! I'm driving! - We're all proud of you, Dad.
jokes.setup: Homer excitedly announces he's driving a go-kart like it's a major achievement
jokes.punchline: Bart's deadpan response treating it like a genuine accomplishment
jokes.explanation: Nice interplay between Homer's childish excitement and Bart's perfectly calibrated sarcastic support.

jokes.text: Uh, are you all right, man? - Uh, I think so. - How about now? - [Screams] Ha-ha!
jokes.setup: Nelson asks if a kid is okay after an apparent accident
jokes.punchline: Nelson immediately attacks him again, revealing it was intentional
jokes.explanation: Perfect Nelson moment - fake concern followed by immediate violence. Great economical structure and character work.

jokes.text: Stand back and watch the pro. - Shouldn't you put on a batting helmet? - Nah. They mess up my hair.
jokes.setup: Nelson calls himself a pro and Bart suggests safety gear
jokes.punchline: Nelson's vanity about his hair overrides basic safety
jokes.explanation: Solid character comedy. Nelson's priorities being backwards is funny and true to character.

jokes.text: Hey! You're goin' down, you- [Groaning] Why, you-Aah! Hey, you- [Grunts] You better-Aah! I'm gonna-Aah! If you do- D'oh!
jokes.setup: Homer tries to threaten someone while being pelted with baseballs
jokes.punchline: His attempts at intimidation are constantly interrupted by getting hit
jokes.explanation: Classic physical comedy escalation. Homer's inability to complete a threat while being pummeled is well-executed.

jokes.text: [Lisa] Yes! A Maggie-oop! [Buzzing] Mm-hmm. - Hey, that's cheating!
jokes.setup: Lisa celebrates Maggie making a basketball shot
jokes.punchline: The pun 'Maggie-oop' (alley-oop) and implication that baby Maggie is cheating somehow
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay with 'Maggie-oop.' The idea that a baby could cheat at arcade basketball adds absurdist humor.

jokes.text: Two thumbtacks and a mustache comb. Or five rubber bands and an ice cube.
jokes.setup: Bart asks what he can get for 12 tickets
jokes.punchline: Ridiculously useless prize combinations that highlight arcade prize futility
jokes.explanation: Brilliant satire of arcade prizes. The specific absurdity of 'ice cube' as a prize is inspired.

jokes.text: What can I get for 8,000 tickets? - A BB gun or an Easy-Bake oven. - Hmm. Hot food is tempting. But I just can't say no to a weapon.
jokes.setup: Bart considers his options between cooking toy and weapon
jokes.punchline: Bart's casual decision-making process favoring violence over food
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor. Bart's casual weighing of 'hot food vs weapon' with weapon winning is perfectly subversive and quotable.

jokes.text: Whoa! Can I try that sometime? - Yeah, sure. Never hurts to have a second set of prints on a gun.
jokes.setup: Bart admires Nelson shooting the BB gun
jokes.punchline: Nelson's casually criminal reasoning about fingerprints
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Nelson line. The casual criminal thinking presented as helpful advice is darkly hilarious and perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Nelson's a troubled, lonely, sad little boy. He needs to be isolated from everyone. - But, Mom- - Yes? - That's all I got.
jokes.setup: Marge describes Nelson sympathetically then prescribes isolation
jokes.punchline: Bart realizes he has no counter-argument to the logical contradiction
jokes.explanation: Good structural comedy. Marge's contradictory logic and Bart's honest defeat create a satisfying comedic beat.

jokes.text: Mustache comb. What'd you get? - Fake mustache. - Wanna comb it? - [Groans] This sucks.
jokes.setup: Reveals they got complementary but useless prizes
jokes.punchline: The cosmic joke of getting perfectly matched worthless items
jokes.explanation: Nice callback to the terrible arcade prizes. The ironic perfection of getting matching useless items is satisfying.

jokes.text: I do what I want when I want. Oh, God! Inchworms!
jokes.setup: Bart declares his rebellious independence
jokes.punchline: Immediately terrified by harmless inchworms
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection. Bart's tough-guy posturing immediately undercut by fear of tiny creatures is classic comedy timing.

jokes.text: My mom's got bigger problems. She doesn't give a crap what I do. - Wow, you are so lucky.
jokes.setup: Nelson explains his mother's neglect
jokes.punchline: Bart envies what's clearly a sad situation
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about parental neglect. Bart's innocent envy of what's actually tragic adds layers.

jokes.text: You're not going out without a scarf, are ya? - Nah, I don't need one. - Hmm. It's your health.
jokes.setup: Nelson shows maternal concern about Bart's scarf
jokes.punchline: The bully demonstrating more parental care than he receives
jokes.explanation: Touching character moment disguised as humor. Nelson showing care while being neglected himself is well-crafted irony.

jokes.text: That's my dad's shootin' car. Just three more payments and it's ours.
jokes.setup: Nelson points out a car full of bullet holes
jokes.punchline: Treating a clearly damaged car as a normal purchase with payment plans
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist humor. The casual treatment of a bullet-riddled car as a normal family vehicle is inspired.

jokes.text: First, how about I try and peg you in the stomach? - Mmm, no, thanks.
jokes.setup: Nelson casually suggests shooting Bart in the stomach
jokes.punchline: Bart's polite but firm decline as if refusing tea
jokes.explanation: Great deadpan response to violence. Bart treating attempted shooting as a polite social offer is perfectly understated.

jokes.text: You're an octo-wussy. 'Whoa, look at me! I'm Bart Simpson! I'm scared to use a gun! I want to marry Milhouse. I walk around like this-' # La la la-la la #
jokes.setup: Nelson mocks Bart for not wanting to shoot a bird
jokes.punchline: Escalates to increasingly ridiculous taunts including wanting to marry Milhouse and effeminate walking
jokes.explanation: Classic bully psychology. Nelson's escalating taunts hitting Bart's specific insecurities is good character work.

jokes.text: Oh, my God. - Whoa! Major shot! You even compensated for the crooked sight.
jokes.setup: Bart horrified at killing the bird
jokes.punchline: Nelson praising his 'skill' and revealing the gun was defective
jokes.explanation: Good dark comedy. Nelson's admiration for what was actually an accident with faulty equipment adds ironic layers.

jokes.text: Ohh! I hate folding sheets. - That's your underwear. - Well, whatever it is, it's a two-man job.
jokes.setup: Homer complains about folding sheets
jokes.punchline: Reveals he can't even identify his own underwear, then doubles down on needing help
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer incompetence. Not recognizing his own underwear as underwear, then insisting it still needs two people, is great escalation.

jokes.text: Milhouse! [Milhouse Shouting] What? Tell Bart to come home! I think he's at Nelson's!
jokes.setup: Homer yells to get information about Bart's whereabouts
jokes.punchline: Milhouse immediately provides the exact information they were trying to hide
jokes.explanation: Solid structural comedy. The immediate undoing of the lie through Milhouse's helpful honesty is well-timed.

jokes.text: Oh, yes, and punish Lisa for lying to us. - All right, young lady. March yourself directly to the Kwik-E-Mart and get me some chips and a beer.
jokes.setup: Marge says to punish Lisa for lying
jokes.punchline: Homer's 'punishment' is sending her on an errand that benefits him
jokes.explanation: Excellent Homer logic. Turning punishment into personal convenience while maintaining authoritative tone is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Get a little somethin' for yourself, sweetheart.
jokes.setup: Homer's fake punishment continues
jokes.punchline: Treats his 'punishment' like a generous parental gesture
jokes.explanation: Good follow-through on the punishment joke. Homer's genuine sweetness while completely missing the point works.

jokes.text: Shall we bury it or chuck it into a car full of girls?
jokes.setup: Nelson suggests disposal options for the dead bird
jokes.punchline: Casual suggestion of traumatizing innocent girls as equal option to burial
jokes.explanation: Perfect Nelson darkness. Treating traumatizing others as a casual disposal method shows his disturbing worldview efficiently.

jokes.text: Relax, Simpson. It was either him or you. No court would convict you.
jokes.setup: Bart feels guilty about killing the bird
jokes.punchline: Nelson frames it as justified self-defense against a bird in court
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic. Nelson's legal analysis of bird vs. human conflict as self-defense is darkly hilarious.

jokes.text: Dear Lord! We just wanted you to put fresh newspaper on the tribunal floor! We're knee-deep in our own droppings. It's disgusting.
jokes.setup: Bart expects to be tried for killing a bird
jokes.punchline: The bird court just wanted janitorial help and admits to poor hygiene
jokes.explanation: Excellent surreal humor. The misdirection from death penalty to custodial complaint is brilliantly absurd.

jokes.text: [Groans] Oww! What are you doing? - I got bored, so I started slapping you.
jokes.setup: Bart wakes up from nightmare being hit
jokes.punchline: Nelson's matter-of-fact explanation for casual violence
jokes.explanation: Perfect Nelson moment. His casual honesty about violence as entertainment is economically brutal and funny.

jokes.text: All right! Finally, a real home! - Not you. Bart.
jokes.setup: Nelson gets excited when Marge says someone's coming home with her
jokes.punchline: Marge clarifies she means Bart, crushing Nelson's hopes
jokes.explanation: Touching dark comedy. Nelson's excitement about a 'real home' reveals his neglect, making Marge's clarification both funny and sad.

jokes.text: What are those cats doing behind you? - [Yowling Continues] Uh, cats like me?
jokes.setup: Cats are mysteriously yowling behind Bart
jokes.punchline: Bart's weak excuse while cats are obviously attracted to something dead he's carrying
jokes.explanation: Solid visual comedy setup. Bart's obvious lie while cats smell the dead bird creates good physical humor.

jokes.text: What's the point, Bart? I punish and I punish and I punish, but it never sinks in. So you know what? Do what you want. You wanna play with little hoodlums? Fine. Have fun killing things.
jokes.setup: Bart expects punishment for his transgression
jokes.punchline: Marge's defeated reverse psychology escalating to encouraging murder
jokes.explanation: Great character moment. Marge's parental exhaustion leading to dark encouragement is both funny and believable.

jokes.text: Hi, little eggs. I'm not sure how to tell you this, but your mom was involved in an incident. Mistakes were made... by me.
jokes.setup: Bart discovers the bird's eggs and needs to explain what happened
jokes.punchline: Corporate-speak euphemisms ('incident,' 'mistakes were made') for murder
jokes.explanation: Brilliant satire. Using corporate/political euphemisms to break news to bird eggs is perfectly absurd and quotable.

jokes.text: You may remember me from such nature films as Earwigs- Eew! and Man vs. Nature- The Road to Victory.
jokes.setup: Troy's standard introduction format
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous nature film titles, especially 'Man vs. Nature- The Road to Victory'
jokes.explanation: Classic Troy McClure. The absurd film titles, especially the anti-environmental 'Road to Victory,' are perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Of course, one thing mother blue jay can't defend against... is a set of steel tongs. [Squawks]
jokes.setup: Describes mother bird's fierce protective instincts
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of human intervention with kitchen utensils
jokes.explanation: Dark nature documentary humor. The casual introduction of human predation with kitchen tools is brilliantly matter-of-fact.

jokes.text: If they're to survive, they require the gentle warmth and tender love... that only a mother can provide. Or better yet, a 75-watt bulb.
jokes.setup: Romantic description of maternal care being irreplaceable
jokes.punchline: Technology is actually superior to motherly love
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection. Building up maternal love then immediately suggesting a light bulb is better is expertly structured.

jokes.text: They look awfully hungry, Mr. McClure. - They sure are, Billy. In nature, their mother would regurgitate food for them to eat. - That's gross! - [Laughs] It sure is, Billy. It sure is.
jokes.setup: Educational discussion about bird feeding habits
jokes.punchline: Troy's enthusiastic agreement that nature is disgusting
jokes.explanation: Good educational TV parody. Troy's unprofessional agreement with the child's disgust breaks documentary tone nicely.

jokes.text: Wouldn't it be easier to just buy one? - Perhaps on a librarian's salary.
jokes.setup: Suggestion that someone buy their own Bible instead of constantly borrowing
jokes.punchline: Librarian's bitter response about low wages
jokes.explanation: Sharp economic humor. The librarian's bitter acknowledgment of their financial reality is well-delivered.

jokes.text: [Clicks] D'oh! [Screaming, Groaning]
jokes.setup: Homer tries to use the heat lamp
jokes.punchline: Gets electrocuted by the defective lamp
jokes.explanation: Standard Homer physical comedy. The repeated electrocution gag is functional but unremarkable.

jokes.text: Check it out, guys. I've been working on this regurgitation thing. [Retching] Oh, yeah! Hope you like Pop-Tarts.
jokes.setup: Bart tries to feed the birds naturally like their mother would
jokes.punchline: His attempt at regurgitation results in Pop-Tarts
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist humor. Bart's attempt at natural feeding resulting in processed food is perfectly ridiculous and quotable.

jokes.text: I think I'll call you Chirpy Boy. And you, BartJunior. And you can call me Mother. No, wait, that sounds kinda fruity. Just call me Mom.
jokes.setup: Bart names the baby lizards and considers what they should call him
jokes.punchline: Self-conscious adjustment from 'Mother' to 'Mom' to avoid sounding 'fruity'
jokes.explanation: Good character moment. Bart's self-consciousness about masculinity while playing mother is believably awkward.

jokes.text: What do you think he's doing up there? - I don't know. Drug lab? - Drug lab? - Or reading comic books. What am I, Kreskin? You tell me what he's doing.
jokes.setup: Marge wonders what Bart is doing upstairs
jokes.punchline: Homer escalates from drug lab to comic books then gets frustrated with his own guessing
jokes.explanation: Good Homer logic. Escalating from serious crime to mundane activity then giving up entirely is typical Homer thinking.

jokes.text: I don't know, and I don't want to know. And I'm going to find out.
jokes.setup: Marge expresses complete lack of interest in Bart's activities
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicts herself by deciding to investigate
jokes.explanation: Perfect parental contradiction. Marge's complete logical reversal in one sentence is economically brilliant.

jokes.text: This is the most exciting thing I've seen since Halley's comet collided with the moon. - That never happened, Dad. - Sure it didn't.
jokes.setup: Homer makes an astronomical comparison
jokes.punchline: Creates fake astronomical event, then refuses correction
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer. Creating false science then stubbornly defending it against his genius daughter is perfect character work.

jokes.text: Hmm. It's starting to cool down. I'll bake another pie. - Ooh, how about cherry this time? - And would it kill you to make some coffee?
jokes.setup: Marge uses pie heat to warm the eggs
jokes.punchline: Family treats the egg-warming as an excuse for food service
jokes.explanation: Good family dynamics. The Simpsons turning altruism into personal benefit is classic character behavior.

jokes.text: Actually, it took 53 hours. - Really? Well, the time just flew by, didn't it?
jokes.setup: Homer claims Bart was born quickly
jokes.punchline: When corrected about the 53-hour labor, says time 'flew by'
jokes.explanation: Good Homer obliviousness. His casual dismissal of Marge's 53-hour ordeal as 'flying by' is perfectly insensitive.

jokes.text: I see a foot. - I see an eye! - I see a neck! - I see a horn! A horn? [All Sigh] [Both Hissing] [All Gasping, Screaming]
jokes.setup: Family excitedly describes hatching birds
jokes.punchline: The 'horn' reveals they're not birds, followed by hissing lizards
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection and escalation. The gradual realization building to horror works perfectly structurally.

jokes.text: Sure, they are. They came from eggs in a bird's nest, therefore they're birds. 'Ickso fatso.'
jokes.setup: Homer defends his bird identification logic
jokes.punchline: Uses made-up Latin phrase to sound authoritative
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer moment. His flawed logic followed by fake Latin ('Ickso fatso') to sound smart is perfect character comedy.

jokes.text: One- they don't have beaks. Two- they don't have feathers. And three- they're lizards! - You're a lizard!
jokes.setup: Lisa provides scientific evidence they're not birds
jokes.punchline: Homer's childish 'you're a lizard' comeback
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer regression. When faced with Lisa's logic, he defaults to playground insults. Very in character.

jokes.text: You cannot count birds that you've seen at the zoo, on stamps or in dreams. - Well, I'm back to square one.
jokes.setup: Strict birding rules are explained
jokes.punchline: Pathetic birdwatcher has apparently only seen birds in invalid contexts
jokes.explanation: Good hobby satire. The implication that someone's entire birding experience consists of invalid sightings is nicely absurd.

jokes.text: My God! A pigeon! That's the last bird on my list. [Chuckles] So long, suckers.
jokes.setup: Desperate birdwatcher finally spots a valid bird
jokes.punchline: It's the most common urban bird, and he immediately abandons the group
jokes.explanation: Nice irony. A serious birder being completed by spotting the most mundane bird possible is good character work.

jokes.text: I'll just get those killed and you can be on your way. - [Gasps] Wait! What the heck are you doing? - My civic duty, that's what.
jokes.setup: Skinner casually offers to kill the lizards
jokes.punchline: Frames mass slaughter as civic responsibility
jokes.explanation: Good Skinner characterization. His bureaucratic enthusiasm for killing combined with moral righteousness is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: My civic duty, that's what. - Wait! What the heck are you doing?
jokes.setup: Expert explains he's doing his civic duty
jokes.punchline: His duty is immediately killing the baby lizards
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about bureaucratic enthusiasm for destruction disguised as civic responsibility

jokes.text: It's already wiped out the dodo, the cuckoo and the ne-ne... and it has nasty plans for the booby, the titmouse, the woodcock and the titpecker.
jokes.setup: List of birds the lizard has supposedly eliminated
jokes.punchline: Escalating to birds with sexually suggestive names
jokes.explanation: Mild innuendo humor. The bird names with sexual connotations are amusing but fairly obvious comedy.

jokes.text: Kill the horrid beasts. Then do away with their lizards.
jokes.setup: Moe demands killing of dangerous creatures
jokes.punchline: Clarifies he means the people first, then their lizards
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Moe moment. The misdirection revealing he wants to kill humans first is darkly perfect and economically delivered.

jokes.text: They must be exterminated as quickly and gruesomely as possible.
jokes.setup: Skinner explains legal requirements for pest control
jokes.punchline: Adds 'gruesomely' to official policy
jokes.explanation: Good Skinner bureaucratic sadism. Adding personal preference for gruesomeness to official policy is in character.

jokes.text: Out of the way, Midge. - Oh, am I in the way? - Yes, yes, you're in the way! Are you daft, woman? - Sorry. I didn't realize I was in the way. - You're still in the way. You don't seem to be moving at all.
jokes.setup: Moe rudely tells Marge to move
jokes.punchline: Marge's overly polite responses while not actually moving create escalating frustration
jokes.explanation: Excellent character interaction. Marge's passive-aggressive politeness versus Moe's direct rudeness creates perfect escalating tension.

jokes.text: Oh, for cryin' out loud, just knock her ass down. - [Moe] Way to shove, Edna. - [Apu] Let us roll.
jokes.setup: Frustration with Marge's blocking reaches peak
jokes.punchline: Multiple characters celebrate finally getting past the polite obstacle
jokes.explanation: Good payoff to the obstruction gag. The group celebration of basic forward movement works as escalation.

jokes.text: Don't use that look on me. I invented that look.
jokes.setup: Someone gives Marge a disapproving look
jokes.punchline: Marge claims ownership of her signature expression
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge moment. Self-aware acknowledgment of her signature disapproving look is both funny and true to character.

jokes.text: You killed 'em! - [Groans] Good riddance to bad lizards.
jokes.setup: Bart mourns what appears to be the lizards' death
jokes.punchline: Skinner's satisfied pun on 'good riddance to bad rubbish'
jokes.explanation: Basic pun humor. Skinner's modification of the common phrase works but isn't particularly inspired.

jokes.text: Go, Chirpy Boy! Go, BartJunior! - Oh, nuts! - Wow. Did you know they had those webbed flaps for gliding? - Yes. But I was hoping they didn't know that.
jokes.setup: Lizards appear to have died but are actually gliding to safety
jokes.punchline: Skinner admits he knew about their gliding ability but hoped they didn't
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection and character moment. Skinner's admission about hoping the lizards wouldn't know their own abilities is absurdly logical.

jokes.text: It seems the rapacious reptiles have developed a taste for the common pigeon... also known as the feathered rat, or gutterbird.
jokes.setup: News report about lizards eating pigeons
jokes.punchline: Increasingly insulting names for pigeons
jokes.explanation: Solid urban wildlife humor. The escalating derogatory terms for pigeons captures real urban sentiment.

jokes.text: No problem. We simply unleash wave after wave of Chinese needle snakes. They'll wipe out the lizards. - But aren't the snakes even worse? - Yes, but we're prepared for that. We've lined up a fabulous type of gorilla that thrives on snake meat. - Then we're stuck with gorillas! - No, that's the beautiful part. When wintertime rolls around, the gorillas simply freeze to death.
jokes.setup: Lisa questions what happens when lizards overpopulate
jokes.punchline: Skinner's increasingly absurd chain of predators ending with gorillas freezing to death
jokes.explanation: Brilliant environmental satire. The escalating absurdity of introducing worse predators, culminating in gorillas freezing to death, is comedy gold. Highly quotable and perfectly structured.

jokes.text: I don't get it, Bart. You got all upset when you killed one bird... but now you've killed tens of thousands and it doesn't bother you at all. - Hey, you're right.
jokes.setup: Lisa points out the moral inconsistency in Bart's reactions
jokes.punchline: Bart's casual acceptance of the logical contradiction
jokes.explanation: Good moral observation. Bart's cheerful acknowledgment of his inconsistent ethics without any plan to change is perfectly in character.
